stories.headline: DA declines homicide charges in Easter Sunday double shooting at Russell Square Park
stories.summary:
The Denver District Attorney's Office has declined to press homicide charges against a 34-year-old male suspect identified in the fatal double shooting at Russell Square Park on Easter Sunday, citing no reasonable likelihood of conviction. Both victims — 43-year-old Sharon Ware and her 18-year-old son Pharrow Ware — died as a result of the April 13 shooting during a family gathering in Denver's Cole neighborhood.

Police said the identified suspect fired his weapon in response to gunfire from a passing stolen vehicle, and investigators believe he struck Sharon Ware while returning fire. Pharrow Ware is believed to have been hit by gunfire from the occupant of the stolen vehicle, which was later found abandoned.

Detectives are continuing to search for the suspects in the stolen vehicle and are working to determine the motive. The DA's office and DPD are exploring the possibility of other charges against the identified suspect, whose custody status remains unclear.

Pharrow Ware's mother, Shy-La Metcalfe, described her son as a 'sweetest, most gentle giant' who was preparing for prom, graduation and college in Utah. Police are urging anyone with information to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Revised data shows Colorado actually lost nearly 12,000 jobs in 2025, worst year since pandemic
stories.summary:
Colorado's economy lost 11,700 non-farm jobs in 2025, according to revised data published last week by the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment — a sharp reversal from initial estimates that had projected a gain of 23,000 jobs. It marks the state's worst employment performance since the pandemic year of 2020.

The private sector shed 7,000 jobs while the public sector lost 4,000. Only two sectors added positions: educational and health services gained 14,200 jobs, and other services grew by 1,900. Sectors recording significant losses included trade, transportation and utilities; leisure and hospitality; manufacturing; and financial activities.

Colorado's labor force shrank by 6,500 people last year, a decline of 0.2%, ranking the state 41st slowest for labor force growth nationally. The annual unemployment rate for 2025 was revised down to 4.0% from an initial estimate of 4.5%, largely because the labor force was shrinking faster than surveys had captured.

The state's unemployment rate ticked up to 3.9% in January 2026, still below the national rate of 4.3%. Economists note that annual job losses of this magnitude are rare outside of recessions or major economic disruptions.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis calls for new Colorado prison as inmate population surges despite falling crime rates
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis is pushing to reopen at least one — and possibly two — closed Colorado prisons to address a ballooning inmate population, even as crime rates in the state have declined. Without additional capacity, the Department of Corrections projects it will need to house more than 100 people on plastic floor cots in a gymnasium.

Experts and advocates attribute the overcrowding not to increased sentencing but to a sharp drop in parole grants and a series of recent laws that have lengthened sentences. A recent DOC audit found that roughly 4,600 incarcerated people — more than one-quarter of the prison population — are past their parole eligibility dates. The state expects new admissions to decrease by 3% this fiscal year.

The situation puts Polis in the uncomfortable position of potentially reversing a campaign promise to end Colorado's contracts with private prison companies. The push for new prison beds comes as lawmakers are simultaneously cutting social services, including health care for low-income residents, to close a $1.5 billion budget gap.

DOC Director Andre Stancil said staffing improvements have reduced a vacancy rate from about 23% in 2023 to around 15% last fiscal year, partly by recruiting workers from Puerto Rico and offering housing to new hires. Critics, including some Democratic legislators, say warnings about overcrowding and understaffing were raised repeatedly but ignored.

stories.headline: Cherry Creek Schools tightens vendor spending policies amid Education Accelerated contract probe
stories.summary:
Cherry Creek Schools is recommending new restrictions on vendor reimbursements to its Board of Education as administrators continue investigating contractor Education Accelerated over potentially overbilled expenses. District officials say Education Accelerated has not provided adequate receipts for tens of thousands of dollars in reimbursed travel and other costs submitted over the past several years.

General counsel Sonja McKenzie wrote in an April 6 email to the company's attorney that the district is 'more than disappointed' with the firm's documentation. The district believes Education Accelerated may have overbilled Cherry Creek by as much as $57,520.97 by routinely exceeding the $5,000 travel expense cap stipulated in its contracts. Education Accelerated's attorney disputed those characterizations, arguing the company has provided substantiating documentation and that the district is retroactively imposing new record-keeping standards.

The broader investigation centers on ties between Education Accelerated and former Superintendent Christopher Smith and his wife, Brenda Smith, the district's former chief human resources officer. Both signed off on nearly $3 million in contracts awarded to the firm; Christopher Smith resigned in January and Brenda Smith was placed on administrative leave shortly thereafter.

If approved, the proposed policy changes would prohibit vendors from receiving reimbursements for out-of-pocket expenses such as travel or meals unless explicitly authorized in a written contract. The school board heard the proposed changes during a public meeting Monday but was not expected to vote on them at that session.

stories.headline: Avalanche fall 3-2 in overtime to Vegas, closing out regular season ahead of playoffs
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche lost 3-2 in overtime to the Vegas Golden Knights at Ball Arena on Saturday night, with Jack Eichel scoring 1:19 into the extra period to clinch the win for Vegas. Devon Toews and Nick Blankenburg scored for Colorado, while goaltender Mackenzie Blackwood stopped 25 of 28 shots.

The loss, while coming late in the regular season, is notable as the Avalanche secured the Presidents' Trophy as the NHL's top regular-season team but must now navigate the playoffs without coach Jared Bednar, who will not travel with the team for the final two road games. Star defenseman Cale Makar has also missed six consecutive games due to injury.

In a positive roster note, forward Jack Ahcan was recalled from AHL Colorado ahead of the postseason, while forward Oskar Brindley was reassigned to the AHL following Thursday's win over Winnipeg. Center Brock Nelson has had a standout individual campaign, adding a milestone to an Olympic gold medal and 30-goal season.

The Avalanche's ability to stay healthy and reintegrate key players will be closely watched as the franchise looks to convert its Presidents' Trophy into a Stanley Cup run — a feat that has historically proven elusive for Presidents' Trophy winners.

stories.headline: Broncos ownership group acquires largest minority stake in Colorado Rockies for $672 million
stories.summary:
Penner Sports Group, the ownership entity led by Denver Broncos owner Greg Penner, has acquired the largest minority ownership stake in the Colorado Rockies in a deal valued at approximately $672 million. The transaction makes the Penner family the most significant outside investor in the MLB franchise.

Rockies owner Charlie Monfort welcomed the investment, saying in a statement: 'Speaking personally, on behalf of Charlie and our family, our other partners and the organization, we're excited to welcome Greg and Carrie Penner into the Colorado Rockies ownership group.' The deal follows an eventful offseason for the Rockies, who hired new president of baseball operations Paul DePodesta and significantly expanded their front office staff.

The Broncos confirmed at the NFL's spring meetings in Arizona that the ownership group would be 'opportunistically aggressive' in pursuing investment opportunities. The Rockies enter the deal sitting 6-10 on the young MLB season, fourth in the National League West, though the team has shown improvement in areas such as base-stealing, ranking fourth in MLB with 17 stolen bases through 16 games.

The investment is seen as a potential stabilizing force for a Rockies franchise that has faced mounting scrutiny over its on-field performance and front-office structure in recent years. The Penner family retains full ownership and operational control of the Broncos.

stories.headline: CU Buffaloes installing new video scoreboard on north side of historic Folsom Field
stories.summary:
The University of Colorado Athletics department is installing a new video scoreboard on the north side of Folsom Field, the 102-year-old stadium in Boulder. The project will also replace all legacy LED boards at the stadium that are 12 years or older.

The new north video board is designed to match the south-side display and ribbon boards installed in 2024. Construction was underway during Saturday's spring football game, and the university expects the new board to be completed and operational in time for commencement ceremonies next month.

The upgrade is part of a broader effort to modernize the stadium's technology infrastructure while preserving the historic venue's character. CU Athletics has been investing in facility improvements in recent years amid heightened interest in the Buffaloes football program.

stories.headline: BLM Colorado oil and gas lease sale generates $8.1 million, most in years, under revised federal royalty rules
stories.summary:
The Bureau of Land Management's quarterly oil and gas lease sale in Colorado generated $8.1 million on March 31, the agency's most successful such sale in recent years. The BLM leased 68 parcels covering more than 42,000 acres across Weld, Jackson, Routt, Arapahoe, Delta, Mesa, Rio Blanco, Gunnison and Garfield counties.

The sale was conducted under reduced royalty rates embedded in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which lowered the onshore federal land oil and gas royalty rate to a minimum of 12.5%, down from 16.67% under the previous administration. The BLM credited the Trump administration's pro-energy production policies, including Executive Order 14154, with spurring the strong results.

Amy Cooke, director of the Energy and Environmental Policy Center at the Independence Institute in Denver, said the surge signals that energy markets are responding to both stronger price conditions and a federal policy shift toward energy abundance. She noted that all leased parcels are on federal land, arguing that Colorado's state-level regulations have made it increasingly difficult to develop new wells on private or state property.

Colorado's energy sector has seen declining production in recent years amid new restrictions enacted by the state legislature and Governor Polis. The federal lease proceeds flow partly back to the state and local governments under revenue-sharing formulas.

stories.headline: Voyager Technologies doubles satellite propulsion output at Littleton facility, eyes further expansion
stories.summary:
Denver-based aerospace company Voyager Technologies has doubled its production capacity for satellite propulsion systems at its Littleton facility compared to a year ago, the company announced Monday. The growth follows Voyager's October acquisition of Littleton satellite technology firm ExoTerra Resource for an undisclosed price.

The Littleton facility has expanded from 8,000 to 40,000 square feet and now employs 200 workers. Voyager said it can produce one satellite propulsion module per day and aims to quadruple capacity from year-ago levels. The systems are designed to help satellites maneuver in orbit and avoid threats.

Demand has been driven in part by the Golden Dome missile defense program, for which Congress allocated $25 billion in last year's Big Beautiful Bill. Voyager CEO Dylan Taylor has said ExoTerra's technology positions the company to win Golden Dome contracts and grow as a leader in electric propulsion for defense and commercial markets.

Voyager is also the developer of Starlab, a private space station intended to replace the International Space Station after the ISS retires. The company's rapid expansion in Littleton underscores Colorado's growing role in the national space defense economy.

stories.headline: Colorado snowpack at historic lows heading into summer, water officials warn of uncharted territory
stories.summary:
Water officials and researchers are sounding alarms about what may be the worst snowpack in Colorado's recorded history as the state heads into the critical spring runoff season. At a presentation in Grand Junction attended by about 200 farmers, water professionals and community members, Colorado River District staff presented graph after graph illustrating dire conditions.

'If there's anything in your memory about a dry year that you've seen, a warm year that you've seen, 2026 is beyond all of that,' said Raquel Flinker, the district's director of interstate and regional water resources. The Colorado River District, along with Airborne Snow Observatories Inc., conducted 18 aerial survey flights in seven days across watersheds from northern to southern Colorado using laser-based lidar technology to measure snowpack with precision.

ASO co-founder Jeff Deems said conditions are stark: 'There's not a lot of snow out there, nor has there been. We've had very little snow and it's melting early.' The data is being used by reservoir operators, city utilities and agricultural water managers to plan how to stretch a severely limited water supply through the summer months.

Mountain snowpack serves as a water tower not just for Colorado but for 19 states and Mexico that depend on Colorado River flows. Officials say the situation is so severe the state is entering territory without precedent in the historical record.

stories.headline: Three bison calves born at Rocky Mountain Arsenal Wildlife Refuge over the weekend
stories.summary:
Staff and volunteers at the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge spotted three newborn bison calves over the course of two days this past weekend, marking the start of calving season at the refuge northeast of Denver. The refuge announced the births on its Facebook page Sunday.

Bison calving season at the refuge typically runs from mid-April through early July, with many births concentrated over several weeks. Newborn calves are born with a reddish-tan coat, making them easier to identify alongside their mothers in the herd.

The refuge is encouraging visitors to come in the next few weeks to see the calves while they are young, while reminding the public to give mothers and their newborns ample space. According to the National Park Service, bison calves begin nursing within 30 minutes of birth and start grazing on grass and drinking water within their first week of life.

stories.headline: Wellness Counter, a new Japanese restaurant centered on dry-aged fish, set to open May 7 in Boulder
stories.summary:
A new Japanese restaurant called Wellness Counter is scheduled to open May 7 at 1117 Pearl St. in Boulder, featuring a dry-aging program for seafood that its owners say can deepen the flavor and alter the texture of fish in ways similar to how aging improves beef or cheese. The restaurant comes from Phoebe and Steven Lee, the couple behind Denver's plant-based Wellness Sushi.

The menu is expected to include nigiri, hand rolls, crudos, robata grill dishes, claypot rice and house-made Japanese milk bread. The beverage program, overseen by Phoebe Lee, will feature green teas sourced from Kyoto, sake, cocktails, wine and coffee.

Steven Lee said the new concept allows him to return to traditional Japanese techniques that the vegan format of Wellness Sushi did not fully accommodate. The couple, originally from Myanmar, describe Wellness Counter as an expansion of the culinary philosophy behind their first restaurant rather than a replacement for it.

Diners can get an early preview during Mile High Asian Food Week, from April 26 through May 1, when Wellness Counter will offer complimentary sago brûlée with each meal. Once fully open, the restaurant will operate seven days a week with service until 10 p.m. on Fridays and Saturdays.

joke.joke: Pie can't compete with cake. Put candles in a cake, it's a birthday cake. Put candles in a pie, someone's drunk in the kitchen.

topStory.headline: Swalwell Announces Congressional Resignation Amid Sexual Misconduct Allegations; Gonzales Also to Retire
topStory.summary:
Rep. Eric Swalwell, D-Calif., announced Monday that he intends to resign from Congress following sexual assault and misconduct allegations that prompted bipartisan calls for his removal and a newly launched House Ethics Committee investigation. In a statement posted to X, Swalwell said he was aware of efforts to bring an immediate expulsion vote against him and cited concern that the controversy would distract him from serving his constituents in California's 14th congressional district. He did not specify an exact date for his departure.

Swalwell apologized to his family, staff and constituents for what he called "mistakes in judgment" while maintaining that at least some of the allegations against him were false. "I will fight the serious, false allegations made against me. However, I must take responsibility for the mistakes I did make," he wrote. His announcement came one day after he suspended his campaign for governor of California, and shortly after Sen. Ruben Gallego, D-Ariz., a close Swalwell ally who chaired his 2020 presidential campaign, said publicly that Swalwell was "no longer fit" to serve in Congress. Former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi said Monday night that Swalwell had made the right decision.

Rep. Tony Gonzales, R-Texas, also announced Monday that he is retiring from Congress after the House Ethics Committee opened a probe into his conduct involving a romantic relationship with a former staffer who later died by suicide. Gonzales, who had already ended his re-election bid last month, said he would file his retirement paperwork when Congress returned. A second former staffer had separately told NBC News this month that Gonzales sent her sexually explicit text messages while she worked for him. Both the Swalwell and Gonzales Ethics Committee investigations will end upon their departures, as the committee holds jurisdiction only over sitting members.

The dual resignations come as Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla., had been organizing expulsion votes targeting Swalwell and Gonzales along with two other lawmakers. Swalwell has represented California's 14th district since 2013. Under California law, Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom will determine whether and when to hold a special election to fill Swalwell's seat, though the timeline remains unclear. Swalwell's exit from the California governor's race further unsettles a Democratic primary already lacking a clear front-runner, with former Rep. Katie Porter and billionaire activist Tom Steyer seen as the leading candidates to consolidate his supporters ahead of an approaching top-two primary.
